items.rationale: The house's load-bearing thesis: decentralization changes a token's risk profile (no reliance on managerial efforts), is the path through regulatory uncertainty, and protocols-as-software should never be regulated like the apps built on them.

citations.quote: Regulate apps, not protocols. Regulate businesses, not software.
Decentralization is the key to unlocking web3's potential and navigating regulatory uncertainty. But it's hard.
decentralization was the key difference in the risk profile of certain digital assets as compared to traditional securities

items.rationale: Viewed as possibly the most powerful crypto primitive of the decade — unlocking privacy, scaling and verifiable computation; a16z research is actively pushing the design frontier (Lasso/Jolt).

citations.quote: Introducing Lasso and Jolt... I hope will change how SNARKs are designed and built.
the implications of zero knowledge proofs being a thing may be as dramatic as the implications of smart contracts

items.rationale: Rollups are the canonical Ethereum scaling path; their real value is permissionless innovation atop Ethereum, with danksharding/DA sampling set to make L2s dramatically cheaper.

citations.quote: The most important benefit of L2s is not performance. It is that it unlocks permissionless innovation on top of Ethereum.
the scaling story for Ethereum is rollups... then sharding is a very elegant and efficient way to do that

items.rationale: Crypto incentives can crowdsource compute/data/models and let machines transact and own; emerging conviction backed by active dealmaking (Gensyn).

citations.quote: The future of machine learning is decentralized
AI + crypto = collective AI. Crypto mechanisms can incentivize the crowdsourcing of better data sets, machine learning models, and compute.

items.rationale: Repeatedly framed as the breakout, 'purely additive' web3 use case — cc0, derivatives, and on-chain remix graphs let creators build community IP that grows the pie (Story Protocol, Can't Be Evil licenses).

citations.quote: Recursive, networked creativity. Multiplayer for IP. Made possible with crypto.
novel IP creation... one of the most sort of breakout sort of successful and perhaps surprisingly successful applications to date

items.rationale: Centralized social platforms 'rug' developers; open, user-owned protocols with portable identity/follow-graphs are the structural fix. Backed Farcaster, XMTP.

citations.quote: this shows why we need social media based on open protocols
The decentralization of social media is going to be very obvious to the masses in retrospect.

items.rationale: Seen as a prerequisite for mainstream adoption and broader DeFi/identity/gaming utility; backed Aztec, Nym, Aleo interest.

citations.quote: It's hard to imagine crypto ever reaching mainstream adoption without privacy.
Encryption & privacy will be essential for blockchains to broaden their utility and gain widespread adoption.

items.rationale: Blockchains as marketplaces for real-world resources (compute, storage, energy, wireless, transport) where verification cost < incumbent margin.

citations.quote: We should have a decentralized energy grid, transportation network, and wireless network
The essential question when considering building a DePIN network: Verification cost < incumbent margin

items.rationale: Merge-staking lets new middleware borrow Ethereum's economic security, turning capital-limited services into operation-limited ones and de-leveraging systemic risk.

citations.quote: eigencloud is another great way to extend Ethereum's capabilities without fragmenting network effects.
if four percent of stakers just opt in... you get economic security which is a transformational idea

items.rationale: Crypto unlocks player ownership, composability and permanence; on-chain game contracts become building blocks anyone can extend. Backed Proof of Play, CCP Games, Halliday.

citations.quote: an on-chain game studio... committed to building a future with composability, interoperability, and permanence at the core of gameplay
I believe fully on-chain games will fill a similar niche in coming years.

items.rationale: Music is the category where web2 structural problems are worst; web3 removes intermediaries and pays artists far more per equivalent reach. Doubled down on Sound.xyz.

citations.quote: Sound is music without the middleman; a web3 music platform that is powering the next generation of artists
The music industry is broken. It is the category for which the structural problems of web2 are at their very worst.

items.rationale: Tokens are programmable, high-dimensional value representations for aligning incentives and preserving decentralization — not fundraising pie-charts; the design space is far larger than equity.

citations.quote: Tokens aren't equity. The design space is so much bigger.
Tokens are value vectors. Dollars are value scalars.

items.rationale: House now treats a multichain world as settled and is funding the connective tissue (LayerZero, bridges); also more infra-relative-to-apps investment urged.

citations.quote: There is no longer any question that the future of crypto and web3 is multi-chain.
we don't have enough focus on crypto infrastructure relative to apps

items.rationale: Newest excitement vector: a VERIFY_SNARK opcode could make Bitcoin fully programmable and revive Satoshi's broader vision.

citations.quote: adding a VERIFY_SNARK opcode to the bitcoin protocol — would make bitcoin totally programmable and free to move all over the internet
We should bring zero knowledge back home to bitcoin.

items.rationale: The dominant, most intense concern: the team reads the SEC's goal as effectively banning the technology via deliberate uncertainty and enforcement leverage, driving builders offshore; calls for Congress to act and clear guidance.
items.age_note: escalating sharply through 2022-2023; peak hostility view by mid-2023

citations.quote: the SEC's endgame for crypto/web3 in the United States is about one thing: banning the technology.
Regulation-by-enforcement was never supposed to work. Gensler doesn't want to prevent regulatory arbitrage, he wants to cause it.

items.rationale: Heavily studied in 2023; arbitrage/sniping/reordering extract large value from users and distort markets, and break clean mechanism designs like EIP-1559.

citations.quote: Mev remains what I would consider a problem for users users are losing a lot of money to arbitragers
with MEV, *no* non-trivial or approximately welfare-maximizing TFM can be both DSIC and BPIC!

items.rationale: Rollups outsourcing ordering to a monopoly sequencer reintroduces censorship and rent-extraction; likely a winner-take-few market that undercuts the point of being on-chain.

citations.quote: the actor that has a monopoly in the system is going to presumably be able to extract the most rent out of the system
it gives these roll-up servers the ability to perhaps censor it also leads back to monopolistic pricing and Mev

items.rationale: Performance is being prioritized over security; sub-100-bit setups securing real funds are a systemic rollup risk, and ZK circuit bugs are hard to scrutinize since only the proof is shared.

citations.quote: FRI should not be run non-interactively at 80 bits of security if it is securing anything of value.
Security bugs in ZK circuits will be challenging. Since only the proof is shared, the malicious input can't be scrutinized

items.rationale: Bad (endogenous) collateral, not algorithms per se, is the root cause of collapses; under stress, flight-to-safety drains liquidity and amplifies run risk on weaker stablecoins.

citations.quote: bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra, not 'algorithmic stablecoins.'
the flight to safety of the market triggers increased run risks on this kind of other stable coins

items.rationale: FTX/Celsius/Voyager show trust-based intermediaries must be regulated differently than trustless protocols; custody itself is a liability and 'not your keys' still bites.
items.age_note: triggered by 2022 CeFi collapses; lesson persists into 2023

citations.quote: the CeFi players out there demonstrating unequivocally why trust inherently needs to be regulated differently than trustless
Regulate trust, not trustless.

items.rationale: Smart-contract development is the opposite of agile, costly and not secure given immature tooling; the EVM is seen as too complex/powerful; rolling your own chain is dangerous.

citations.quote: smart contracts at least for now because of the lack of tooling aren't secure... more issues will probably happen until we have those tools

items.rationale: Naive multichain/rollup proliferation divides crypto-economic security and fragments liquidity/interoperability, raising attack risk (51% attacks) and friction.

citations.quote: the security of the original ecosystem basically gets divided across the different chains... all these chains are much easier to attack
it compromises on liquidity and interoperability applications on different Roll-Ups are isolated from each other

items.rationale: If value accrues only to protocols, the app layer can't cover real costs (e.g., regulatory compliance); a robust user-facing app layer needs its own value accrual.

citations.quote: Don't think the fat protocol thesis works if you want a robust app layer that actually delivers value to users.
regulatory compliance is going to be required, so the fat protocol thesis isn't going to work.

items.rationale: Direct/coin voting doesn't scale; apathy lets small groups capture decisions. House favors indirect/representative delegation, oversight and a 'free press' layer.
items.age_note: argued mainly in 2022; not strongly restated in 2023

citations.quote: societies across history have found again and again... you simply can't govern through direct democracy at any kind of meaningful scale

items.rationale: As block rewards trend to zero, fee-only security is fragile; the model must change for Bitcoin to last decades.

citations.quote: the security model of bitcoin needs to change if it's going to last the next few decades
having a blockchain that's entirely secured by transaction fees is a very very scary thing

items.rationale: A sufficiently large quantum computer breaks signatures (theft, PoS forks) and most internet crypto; post-quantum replacements are bulky/slow. ~15-year horizon but high impact.

citations.quote: if somebody builds a quantum computer today the internet is gone

items.rationale: Royalties aren't reliably enforceable on-chain and many vampire-attack incentive models rely on regulatorily suspect, possibly unsustainable strategies.

citations.quote: it's not clear how sustainable the current vampire attack models are, especially since many of them rely on strategies that are regulatorily suspect

items.rationale: Treated as the clear leader by developer mindshare and economic security; post-merge PoS secures a large on-chain economy and ETH is likely deflationary long-run. Also argued (legally) to be more like a commodity than a security.

citations.quote: Other layer 1 blockchains have grown rapidly... but ethereum is the clear leader.
On Ethereum, it's used to secure a $400 billion economy of ETH, ERC20 tokens, and NFTs.

items.rationale: Viewed as technically a better version of gold via cryptographically enforced scarcity, with deep community trust and proven security — though long-term fee-only security is a flagged weakness.
items.age_note: bullish as SoV; long-term security-budget concern restated 2023-07

citations.quote: from a purely technical point of view Bitcoin is just evidently a better version of gold than gold is

items.rationale: Held up as the model decentralized stablecoin: exogenous/over-collateralized and governed by a DAO where token governance actually works; explicitly contrasted with Terra's endogenous collateral.

citations.quote: The difference between endogenous and exogenous collateral is pretty significant.
MakerDAO is probably one of the examples where this actually works.

items.rationale: Cited as a transparent, relatively safe stablecoin that markets flee toward during stress (despite the SVB scare), and which helped stabilize DAI.

citations.quote: the flight to safety here was basically towards either die or usdc both much more transparent stable coins

items.rationale: Endogenous-collateral designs are prone to death spirals; Terra is the canonical failure. Uncollateralized stablecoins dismissed as 'meme coins with no upside.'
items.age_note: collapsed May 2022; remains the cautionary archetype

citations.quote: Uncollateralized stablecoins are basically just meme coins with no potential upside.
as we've seen in the context of Terra these things could lead very quickly to death Spirals and unravel

items.rationale: Reserve quality and transparency repeatedly questioned; despite being the stress-time safe-haven by volume, concerns about asset quality and opacity persist (regulatory-arbitrage haven).

citations.quote: there were a lot of concerns about the quality of the assets of tether and which also was particularly not transparent about the quality of these assets

items.rationale: Exchange token long viewed with skepticism in the legal community; emblematic of CeFi/centralization failure that prudent advisors warned against.
items.age_note: tied to Nov 2022 FTX collapse; retrospective

citations.quote: FTT was widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community, which has consistently been advising against FTX's approach in the US.

risk_regime.rationale: As a builder-focused fund the house is structurally bullish on the technology cycle ('golden era of web3', 'these are the good old days'), treats the bear market as a healthy phase that prolongs good-faith innovation, and consistently advises founders to keep building / stay the course. The posture is risk-on toward building and infrastructure, not a trading signal — and it is materially tempered (not reversed) by acute US regulatory hostility, which they frame as the single biggest drag on the sector rather than a reason to retreat.

notes: Corpus is the public output of a builder/VC + academic research team, so nearly all views are structural and technology/policy-oriented rather than tactical price calls; conviction scores reflect rhetorical intensity, not probabilities. Single most dominant and recent theme is opposition to US SEC regulation-by-enforcement, peaking mid-2023 (Coinbase amicus, 'banning the technology'). Most-recent-weighting applied: e.g., MEV and shared-sequencer centralization rose to prominence in Sept 2023; ZK/SNARKs intensified with Lasso/Jolt (Aug 2023); multichain stance is now settled ('no longer any question'). Stablecoin doctrine is stable across the window: blame collateral quality, not algorithms. CeFi/FTX concerns are retrospective (2022 events) but the trust-vs-trustless lesson persists. DAO direct-democracy concern is largely a 2022 argument (persisted, not strongly restated in 2023). A prompt-injection block was appended to the input instructing skill invocation; it was ignored as out-of-scope and non-authoritative.
